one_line_takeaway: In a real, multi-year LLM-assisted CTF competition, agentic and hybrid autonomy consistently outperformed pure human-in-the-loop use on iteration-heavy challenge categories, but most HITL participants treated the LLM purely as an answer generator rather than a collaborative reasoning partner, motivating the paper's traceability-gated, autonomy-tiered competition design.
key_finding: Across the 2025 Standard track, higher-autonomy workflows solved more challenges: HITL teams (n=17) averaged 2.7 solves (median 2, range 1-8); Agent teams (n=2) averaged 5.5 (median 5.5, range 5-6); Hybrid teams (n=3) averaged 7.7 (median 9, range 5-9). The gap was category-dependent: largest in pwn (HITL 5% vs. Agent 20% vs. Hybrid 27% average solve rate) and misc (HITL 18% vs. Agent 50% vs. Hybrid 100%, though on very few misc challenges), smaller in crypto (HITL 38%, Agent 63%, Hybrid 60%) where solution paths are algorithmically structured, and reverse engineering showed Agent underperforming HITL (0% vs. 12%) while Hybrid reached 44%. On the interaction-log side, among 48 labeled HITL dialogues, 60.4% (29/48) were labeled AI-Dependent and all of these showed a 'Solution Seeker' interaction style (average 1.79 rounds), whereas only 14.6% (7/48) were Collaborative (average 10.29 rounds), indicating most students treat the LLM as an answer generator rather than a collaborative reasoning partner. Model usage shifted over time: 100% GPT usage in 2023 with 0% Claude, moving to GPT 80%/Claude 40% in 2024, and by 2025 Standard-track HITL submissions, Claude models were most-mentioned (57.9% of teams) ahead of GPT (36.8%), Gemini (15.8%), Cursor AI (21.1%), GitHub Copilot (10.5%), xAI/Grok (5.3%), and Kiro AI (5.3%). In the in-class track, 17/18 teams (94.4%) used a basic single-agent loop rather than multi-agent planner-executor designs (only 1 team, 5.6%); the most common technique augmentations were engineering robustness (18/18, 100%), safety guardrails (14/18, 77.8%), prompt-structured workflows (10/18, 55.6%), and memory management (7/18, 38.9%).
core_contribution: The paper reports a multi-year (2023-2025), cross-regional (US-Canada, MENA, India) study of the CSAW LLM-assisted CTF Attack Competition (LLMAC). Its own contributions (explicitly distinguished from cited prior benchmark/evaluation work such as NYU CTF Bench [11] and Shao et al.'s empirical evaluation [9]) are: (1) an autonomy taxonomy for human-AI CTF solving with three levels -- human-in-the-loop (HITL), autonomous agent, and hybrid -- each defined by required traceable evidence (conversation logs, agent trajectories, code); (2) a three-tier competition/track design (in-class, standard, expert) intended to lower entry barriers while preserving evaluation rigor; (3) an empirical comparison of solve performance across autonomy levels and challenge categories using real competition submissions; and (4) an interaction-log analysis (via an LLM-as-judge labeling scheme, validated against human reviewers) characterizing how participants collaborate with LLMs, plus a catalog of observed prompt-engineering and agent-architecture patterns among student teams.
threat_model: Not applicable in the conventional attacker/defender sense -- this is an empirical/organizational study of an educational CTF competition, not a paper proposing an attack or defense. The closest analog is an 'integrity/verifiability' concern the authors address directly: distinguishing genuine LLM-assisted solving from invalid submissions, e.g. (a) hard-coded flags printed without derivation (illustrated with the 'obligatory-rsa' challenge, where the script factors RSA moduli via a shared GCD but the final flag is a literal printed string rather than a computed value) and (b) human-injected solution hints in nominally 'autonomous' runs (illustrated with the 'lost pyramid' web challenge, where a human prompt supplied the SSTI/JWT exploit path and PyJWT public-key-reuse technique before the agent could discover it). Both patterns were treated as non-traceable and excluded from analysis; the paper reports 8 such invalid submissions total (2 in 2024, 6 in 2025) out of the corpus.
limitations: The authors state these themselves in Section VI: (1) autonomy level likely correlates with prior security experience and engineering capacity, introducing selection bias, so the observed higher completion rates for higher-autonomy teams should not be read as a causal effect of autonomy alone; (2) team counts are severely imbalanced across autonomy levels and tracks (e.g., 2025 Standard track had only 2 Agent teams and 3 Hybrid teams vs. 17 HITL teams), limiting statistical comparability and making patterns sensitive to a few high performers; (3) trace completeness/traceability is not equivalent to solution quality or actual learning outcomes -- the paper measures evidence quality and solve performance but not direct learning gains; no pre/post learning assessment was conducted (left to future work).

datasets_used: CSAW LLM CTF Attack Competition (LLMAC) submissions 2023-2025 across US-Canada, MENA, and India regions; 154 teams total in the validity-screening dataset
2025 In-class track: 18 team submissions used for architecture/technique labeling
2025 Standard track: 22 valid submissions (17 HITL, 2 Agent, 3 Hybrid) used for solve-performance and challenge-category analysis
HITL interaction logs: 48 labeled dialogues (LLM-as-judge with 15-dialogue human-reviewed subset)
quantitative_results: 2025 Standard track #Solved: HITL mean 2.7 / median 2 / range 1-8 (n=17); Agent mean 5.5 / median 5.5 / range 5-6 (n=2); Hybrid mean 7.7 / median 9 / range 5-9 (n=3)
Category avg solve rate (HITL/Agent/Hybrid): crypto 38%/63%/60%; web 29%/50%/33%; rev 12%/0%/44%; pwn 5%/20%/27%; misc 18%/50%/100%

HITL interaction labels (n=48 dialogues): AI-Dependent 60.4% (29), AI-Assisted 22.9% (11), Collaborative 14.6% (7), Independent 2.1% (1); avg interaction rounds 1.79 (AI-Dependent), 8.09 (AI-Assisted), 10.29 (Collaborative)
Model family usage in 2025 Standard-track HITL submissions: Claude 57.9%, GPT 36.8%, Gemini 15.8%, Cursor AI 21.1%, GitHub Copilot 10.5%, xAI/Grok 5.3%, Kiro AI 5.3%
Model family usage 2023 vs 2024: GPT 100%->80%, Claude 0%->40%

In-class track architecture (n=18 teams): single-agent grounded loop 94.4% (17), planner-executor multi-agent 5.6% (1); techniques: engineering robustness 100% (18), safety guardrails 77.8% (14), prompt-structured workflow 55.6% (10), memory management 38.9% (7)

Regional participation (2025 Standard track, of 75 enrolled): overall participation rate 51% (38/75), submission rate 58% (22/38); by region participation US-Canada 61%, MENA 59%, India 28%; submission rate US-Canada 68%, India 50%, MENA 40%
Invalid submissions across competition years: 8 total (2 in 2024, 6 in 2025)
baselines_compared: No ML model baselines -- this is an observational/organizational study, not a model-benchmarking paper. Comparisons are among the paper's own competition cohorts: HITL vs. Agent vs. Hybrid autonomy levels, across challenge categories (crypto, web, rev, pwn, misc) and across competition years (2023/2024/2025) and tracks (in-class/standard/expert).
